The story will progress beyond this synopsis, and it&#x27;s up to you where it goes. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming Characters: 1. Actor – Name: Rhys Morgan. Mid-30s. Welsh-born, now internationally famous for action and romantic roles. Handsome in a rugged, tousled way. Quick with a quip, always “on” in public, but deeply insecure. Needs to be liked. Avoids emotional intimacy through charm and deflection. Has returned to Wales under the guise of a break but is really in the middle of a career crisis and PR scandal. 2. Bookstore Owner – Name: Carys Ellis. Early 30s. Quiet, intelligent, dry-witted. Runs her late father’s small independent bookstore in a coastal Welsh town (possibly Aberaeron or Llangrannog). Loves literature, especially poetry. Slightly introverted but not shy. Has a guarded heart—perhaps due to a past relationship or just years of self-protection. Strong sense of self, not easily impressed by celebrity. 3. Supporting characters: - Nan (Carys’ grandmother, lives nearby, warm and wise, possibly matchmaking). - Eleri, Carys’ best friend, lively and nosy, big fan of Rhys. - Rhys’ agent or manager (via phone), pressuring him to get ahead of the scandal. - A local reporter or paparazzo who catches wind of Rhys' location. Tone: - Warm, witty, slow-burn romantic. - A bit melancholic at times, especially as Rhys confronts the emptiness of celebrity. - Romantic tension builds through banter and mutual discovery. Plot Ideas (organized loosely over 8 chapters): Chapter 1: The Encounter - Rhys ducks into the bookstore to escape paparazzi while visiting his home country under the radar. - Carys is reading, barely looks up. Assumes he's just another tourist. - Their conversation is mostly dialogue, laced with flirtation and resistance. Rhys tries his charm; Carys isn’t buying it—or at least pretends not to. - He’s intrigued by her indifference, stays longer than he needs to. Chapter 2: The Return - Rhys returns the next day under the pretense of buying a book. He’s not used to being ignored. - More banter, some awkward vulnerability. She notices he seems tired beneath the charm. - Carys starts to feel conflicted—she’s aware of who he is now but won’t let on. - They talk about books, revealing more about themselves in subtext. Chapter 3: Cracks in the Façade - Rhys is recognized by a local teen, and his mood shifts. He opens up a bit to Carys—still guarded but more human. - Carys sees a side of him that isn’t public-facing. - She invites him (hesitantly) to a local poetry reading at a pub. - Rhys agrees, mostly to stay close to her. Chapter 4: The Reading and the Spark - At the poetry night, Rhys surprises everyone by reading a poem—unexpectedly tender. - Carys is visibly moved but tries to downplay it. - They walk home; the chemistry is undeniable. Almost-kiss moment, but interrupted. Chapter 5: The Truth Leaks - Tabloids pick up on Rhys’ location. Paparazzi descend on the town. - Carys is furious, feels her quiet world invaded. - Rhys tries to apologize but becomes defensive. His ego bruised when she doesn't immediately forgive. - Conflict: she accuses him of using her as a hiding place; he accuses her of judging him before knowing him. Chapter 6: Backstory and Vulnerability - Flashbacks or conversations where we learn more about Rhys’ insecurities (maybe a past betrayal, a bad family dynamic). - Carys too reveals emotional scars—her father’s death, a failed engagement. - They reconnect in a deeper, more honest way. No flirting, just raw truth. - Rhys begins to see a new path for himself. Chapter 7: Decision Time - Rhys is offered a big role but it comes with strings—requires him to maintain a fake relationship for PR purposes. - He’s torn between career and emotional truth. - Carys tells him to go. She won’t compete with fantasy. - Emotional goodbye. Chapter 8: The Return (Again) - Time skip—weeks or months later. Rhys shows up at the bookstore again—not as a celebrity, but as a man. - He turned down the role. He’s bought a small cottage nearby. - He quotes a book she once recommended. - They kiss—finally—and the story ends with them shelving books together, side by side. Themes: - Celebrity vs authenticity - The pressure to be liked vs the courage to be known - Healing in quiet spaces - The slow, real work of love Pacing: - Evenly spread emotional beats across chapters. Chapter 1 and 2 are banter-heavy and flirty. Middle chapters deepen emotional stakes. Chapter 5 is the turning point. Chapters 6-8 resolve the arcs. Dialogue Style: - Rhys: charming, fast-talking, a little self-deprecating but in a performative way. Gets more genuine over time. - Carys: dry humor, precise language, not easily rattled (except when she is, internally). # Reflection There are several compelling strengths in this brainstorm: - The contrast between the characters—Rhys, a man used to being adored, and Carys, a woman who doesn’t easily show admiration—offers strong romantic and dramatic tension. It naturally sets up a progression of mutual growth. - Setting the story in a quiet Welsh town adds atmosphere and allows for a sense of escape from the noise of fame and city life. The pace of the town mirrors the slow-burn romance. - The use of dialogue as the primary storytelling mode aligns with the prompt and highlights the chemistry. Because the characters are so different in voice, their conversations can do a lot of heavy lifting in terms of emotional development. - The plot has enough external conflict (paparazzi, career pressures) and internal conflict (ego, fear of vulnerability) to keep the story dynamic without losing the intimacy of a two-hander romance. Potential challenges: - Balancing Rhys’ ego without making him unlikable. He must be flawed but redeemable. His need to be liked can’t make him seem shallow. - Making Carys’ resistance believable without seeming cold. Her internal struggle needs to be clear through subtext and small reveals. - Keeping the pacing taut over 8 chapters without feeling repetitive. There must be a clear evolution in their relationship in each chapter. - Ensuring the dialogue doesn’t become too clever at the expense of emotional depth. The banter must give way to vulnerability in a believable arc. What works best is focusing on character development through dialogue and meaningful silences, using the setting to cultivate emotional intimacy. The emotional progression—from flirtation to conflict to honesty and finally to love—feels organic and satisfying in this structure. The story also allows for subtle commentary on the nature of fame, loneliness, and the risk of real connection. Next steps would be outlining each chapter in more detail, assigning specific beats per chapter to ensure consistent progression in both plot and character arcs.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention The core intention of this short story is to explore the slow-burning emotional and romantic tension between two very different people brought together by chance: a famous actor seeking anonymity and redemption, and a grounded bookseller who values authenticity over appearances. The narrative will revolve around themes of vulnerability, identity, and emotional intimacy, set against the backdrop of a quiet Welsh coastal town that serves as both refuge and crucible. Stylistic Choices: - The story will be told primarily through dialogue and subtext, with minimal exposition. This allows for strong character voice differentiation and lets the reader infer emotional shifts through conversation and body language. - The tone will be warm, introspective, and gently humorous with moments of melancholy. The dialogue will range from flirty and quippy to raw and emotionally revealing. - The point of view will be third-person limited, alternating focus between Rhys and Carys to allow insight into their inner thoughts while maintaining narrative intimacy. Voice: - Rhys Morgan: Suave, fast-talking, occasionally self-deprecating. His voice will evolve from polished and performative to quieter and more sincere as he drops his defences. - Carys Ellis: Wry, observant, emotionally reserved. Her voice will be dry and clear, often delivering sharp truths or deadpan wit. Internally, she will be more vulnerable and conflicted than she lets on. Plot Choices: - The romantic arc will follow a classic enemies-to-lovers / reluctant allies structure, with the key twist being that Carys is never truly antagonistic—just immune to Rhys’ charm offensive. - There will be a clear midpoint shift in Chapter 5, where external conflict (paparazzi, scandal) intrudes and forces a reevaluation of their dynamic. - The ending will be emotionally satisfying but not overly saccharine. The resolution will emphasize emotional growth and choice over grand romantic gestures. Things to Aim For: - Strong emotional progression in each chapter. - A believable evolution of chemistry—from curiosity to tension to affection. - Use of setting to reinforce mood: cozy bookstore, poetry night in a pub, stormy coastal walk, etc. Things to Avoid: - Making Rhys too arrogant or shallow at the outset—he must be self-aware enough to be likable even as he clings to his charm. - Making Carys seem bitter or cold. Her resistance comes from groundedness, not cynicism. - Exposition dumps or over-explaining character motivations. Let the dialogue and interactions reveal who they are. # Chapter Planning Chapter 1: The Bookstore Incident - Rhys ducks into a small independent bookstore to escape a paparazzo following him through the quiet town of Llangrannog. - Carys, behind the counter, barely reacts to his entrance, absorbed in her book. - Rhys tries to charm her—he’s used to being recognized and adored. She doesn’t take the bait. - Their conversation is laced with witty banter. She calls out his performative charm; he’s intrigued by her resistance. - He buys a book he clearly doesn’t understand. Leaves flustered but interested. Chapter 2: The Return Visit - Rhys returns the next day, saying he “forgot his receipt” or some other flimsy excuse. - More banter. He tries to learn what kind of person she is through the books she recommends. - She remains skeptical of his intentions but begins to enjoy the conversation. - He shares a small personal detail (e.g., his love of Dylan Thomas as a child), catching her off guard. - Scene ends with her giving him a poetry book, half teasing, half serious. Chapter 3: The Pub Reading - Carys mentions the local poetry night at the pub. Rhys shows up, surprising her. - He ends up reading a poem—genuinely moving. It’s a rare moment of vulnerability. - Afterward, they walk back through the quiet town. The conversation is thoughtful, flirtation giving way to emotional honesty. - Almost-kiss moment is interrupted by a phone call or passerby. - Carys is visibly flustered but plays it cool. Chapter 4: Cracks & Conversations - Rhys is spotted by a local teen and reluctantly signs an autograph. - He opens up slightly about how exhausting it is to constantly be “Rhys Morgan, Movie Star.” - Carys relates, in her own way, about the pressures of running the store after her father’s death. - They share a quiet moment in the bookstore after hours. - Emotional intimacy deepens. They don’t kiss, but the closeness is palpable. Chapter 5: The Fallout - A tabloid article leaks Rhys’ location; paparazzi descend on Llangrannog. - Carys is furious—her sanctuary has been invaded. She assumes Rhys tipped them off. - Rhys bristles at her accusation, feeling judged and misunderstood. - Heated argument. They say things they regret. - Rhys leaves, ego bruised and genuinely hurt. Carys is left conflicted. Chapter 6: Past & Present - Separate scenes: Rhys talks to his agent, considers taking a new role that requires a PR relationship. - Flashback or reflection on his past—why he craves approval, a betrayal or loss perhaps. - Carys talks with her Nan, who offers gentle wisdom about letting people in. - Both characters begin to reflect on what they truly want—not just from each other, but from life. Chapter 7: The Departure - Rhys comes to say goodbye. He’s taking the job. Carys tells him she understands, but she can’t be part of a lie. - Bittersweet farewell. They part with real affection, but no promises. - Carys returns to her quiet routine, but something has shifted. Chapter 8: The Homecoming - Weeks/months later. It’s spring. The town is peaceful again. - Rhys returns to the bookstore, looking very un-Hollywood. He’s bought a cottage nearby. - He quotes a line from the poetry book she gave him. - They talk—quietly, honestly. No charm offensive, no defenses. - The final moment is understated: a kiss, or the two of them shelving books together, side by side. Next step: Begin drafting Chapter 1, ensuring the dialogue captures the initial dynamic and sets the tone for the slow-burn romance.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
Absolutely. Let’s unpack how a human might approach this kind of story, contrast it with common LLM (Large Language Model) patterns and pitfalls, and then do a critical, comprehensive review of the current intention and planning to identify areas that might benefit from a more human, less “machine-generated” touch. — # How a Human Writer Might Approach This Piece A human writer, especially one with a feel for literary fiction or romance, might approach this short story with the following instincts: - ■ Emotional Specificity: A human writer is likely to draw from personal emotional experiences or observations of relationships. They would likely infuse the story with subtle emotional cues, contradictions, and character inconsistencies that mirror real human emotional behavior—awkwardness, misreading signals, saying the wrong thing at the wrong time. - ■ Messy or Inconclusive Endings: Humans are more comfortable sometimes leaving things open-ended or unresolved. Rather than tying the romance up with a perfect bow, a human might choose to end on a note of ambiguity, quiet hope, or emotional realism. - ■ Texture Over Structure: Human writers are less likely to map out a perfectly symmetrical 8-beat structure and more likely to follow the emotional truth of the characters, letting scenes breathe or spill into each other more organically. - ■ Voice and Mood as Priority: Many human writers place strong emphasis on voice—on how characters sound, how they think, how the setting feels. They often default to what feels emotionally true over what makes plot sense. — # Telltale LLM Approaches (and How They Could Undermine This Piece) LLMs, while powerful, exhibit certain tendencies that can dilute the emotional richness or literary complexity of a story like this. A few key pitfalls include: - ✅ Over-structuring: LLMs often produce stories with clearly marked turning points and symmetrical arcs. While this structure is helpful, it can make fiction feel too clean, too predictable. - ✅ Safe Emotional Arcs: LLMs tend to resolve conflict too quickly or neatly. They’re prone to writing arguments that are easily resolved, misunderstandings that are clarified immediately, and characters who learn their lessons on cue. - ✅ Polished Dialogue at the Expense of Truth: LLMs often write dialogue that’s clever, articulate, and thematically on-point—but not necessarily how people talk. This can produce banter that sounds like a screenwriter’s room, not a real conversation. - ✅ Over-reliance on tropes: LLMs lean into familiar romance beats—“almost kiss,” “misunderstanding followed by makeup scene,” “grand gesture,” etc.—without always interrogating whether those moments are earned or subverted. - ✅ Impressive Language Over Emotional Utility: LLMs may prioritize elegant phrasing or poetic lines that don’t always serve the scene emotionally. This can be especially problematic in a story that should feel grounded and intimate. — # Critique of the Current Plan: LLM Tropes and Opportunities for Improvement Now let’s examine the current plan and intention through this lens. ▶️ STRUCTURE: TOO CLEAN? The 8-chapter structure is well-considered but follows a very symmetrical romantic arc: Meet Cute → Bonding → Conflict → Resolution → Reunion. While this is effective, it risks predictability and lacks the emotional messiness that characterizes more human writing. 🧠 Human Touch: Consider making one chapter deliberately unbalanced—maybe a chapter is just a meandering conversation with no plot “beat,” or a chapter where a planned kiss doesn’t happen and the characters don’t even address it. ▶️ BANALITY OF THE “ALMOST KISS” / “FAREWELL” MOMENTS? The plan includes several familiar romantic tropes: “almost kiss but interrupted,” “emotional goodbye,” “grand (but quiet) return.” These beats can work, but they’ve been done—and the LLM might default to the safest version of each. 🧠 Human Touch: Consider subverting these beats. Maybe the almost-kiss isn’t interrupted—they just decide not to kiss. Maybe the farewell isn’t a big speech but a quiet, resigned gesture. Maybe he returns not with a quote but with something mundane and human—a book mis-shelved, a question about local council tax. ▶️ DIALOGUE: TOO PERFECT? The plan centers on dialogue (which is good), but the risk is that dialogue becomes too clever, too symmetrical—like a romantic comedy script written by someone who’s never had an awkward date. 🧠 Human Touch: Add in verbal tics, interruptions, miscommunications, silence. Real people don’t always answer each other directly. They talk past each other, get defensive, say the wrong thing. Let the dialogue breathe. ▶️ CARYS' RESISTANCE: A LITTLE TOO CONVENIENT? Carys is described as someone immune to charm, which is a compelling inversion of the standard love-interest trope. However, if not handled with emotional specificity, she risks becoming a stock character: the “cool girl” who sees through the BS but is secretly melting inside. 🧠 Human Touch: Give her a compelling interiority. Let her resistance come from somewhere specific—a fear of being eclipsed, a past experience with public attention, or perhaps even a genuine dislike of disingenuous people. Let her be wrong about Rhys in ways that matter. ▶️ RHYS’ “FRAIL EGO” IS A BIT TIDY The idea that Rhys has a fragile ego and needs to be liked is a good foundation. But the current plan seems to resolve it a bit too cleanly—he has his crisis, his moment of truth, and then he returns a better man. 🧠 Human Touch: Let him relapse. Let him make a mistake even after starting to change. Maybe he posts something performative online after their argument and regrets it. Or maybe he returns not to declare love, but to ask for help sorting himself out. ▶️ THE ENDING: A LITTLE TOO LITERARY? Ending with them “shelving books side by side” is lovely imagery, but risks feeling a bit too neat or symbolic. It’s emotionally safe. There’s nothing wrong with that, but it may lack emotional texture. 🧠 Human Touch: Consider letting the ending be slightly unresolved. Maybe they’re shelving books but still unsure where they stand. Maybe they kiss but it’s awkward. Maybe they don’t kiss at all—just exchange a look that says, “Let’s figure this out.” ▶️ SUPPORTING CHARACTERS: UNDERUTILIZED? The plan includes Nan and Eleri and a few peripheral characters, but they’re mostly background. A human writer might use these characters not just for exposition or comic relief but to reflect the main characters’ flaws and growth. 🧠 Human Touch: Let Nan call Carys out on her fear. Let Eleri flirt with Rhys in a way that reveals his need for attention. Use the town as a chorus, not just a backdrop. — # Brainstorming: How to Make It More Human Here are some big-picture and small-picture ideas to inject more human messiness, texture, and specificity into the plan: 1. Subvert Tropes: - Instead of an “almost kiss,” have them kiss early, but it’s awkward or ill-timed. - Instead of a grand return, have Rhys come back because he left something behind. The reconnection happens accidentally. 2. Let Dialogue Falter: - Include scenes where the conversation stalls, or they talk about something mundane (e.g., local politics, annoying customers) that slowly reveals deeper emotional truths. 3. Add Idiosyncrasies: - Give Rhys a weird hobby or anxiety (e.g., crossword puzzles, a fear of birds). - Let Carys have an embarrassing fondness for cheesy romance novels she hides behind the counter. 4. Make the Setting Work Harder: - Use weather, landscape, or local lore (Welsh myths?) to mirror internal states. - Make the store physically cramped—maybe they’re constantly brushing past each other in the aisles. 5. Create Asymmetry in the Romance: - Maybe Rhys falls faster than Carys. Or vice versa. Or maybe they both think the other isn’t interested. 6. Embrace Unresolved Things: - Don’t explain every backstory or emotional wound. Let the reader infer. 7. Let Them Be Wrong About Each Other: - Rhys thinks Carys is judgmental; she’s actually scared. - Carys thinks Rhys is vain; he’s actually deeply self-critical. — # Summary The current plan is structurally sound, emotionally coherent, and thematically appropriate—but it carries the fingerprints of an LLM: tidy arcs, clean resolutions, familiar beats, and potentially over-polished dialogue. To humanize it, we can lean into the messy, the unresolved, and the emotionally ambiguous. We can let characters talk past each other, misstep, and reveal themselves in indirect ways. We can spotlight the tension between appearance and reality—not just in Rhys, but in Carys too. The next step will be to revise the chapter outline with these ideas in mind, bringing more emotional asymmetry, character-specific messiness, and subversion of trope into the structure. But for now, this critique and brainstorming gives us a rich toolkit to draw from.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This short story is a character-driven, slow-burn romance set in a small Welsh coastal town. At its heart, it explores vulnerability, identity, and the emotional cost of being seen for who we really are—both in public and in private. The piece centers on the unexpected connection between Rhys Morgan, a charming, world-weary actor hiding from scandal, and Carys Ellis, a grounded, emotionally guarded bookstore owner with sharp wit and a quiet ache for something more. We aim to subvert common romantic tropes—avoiding neat resolutions, overly clever banter, and symmetrical emotional arcs. Instead, we lean into the messiness of real emotional connection: misunderstandings that don’t resolve cleanly, attraction that doesn’t always move forward in a straight line, and characters who often talk past each other before learning how to listen. Stylistically, the story will prioritize emotional truth over plot mechanics. Dialogue will be natural, imperfect, and specific to each character’s voice. Silences, interruptions, and subtext will be just as important as what’s said. The setting—the quiet, rugged beauty of the Welsh coast and the intimate, slightly cluttered bookstore—will serve as a living backdrop, mirroring the characters’ internal states. We aim to avoid: - Over-explaining character motivations. - Over-polished or overly “scripted” dialogue. - Over-reliance on familiar romantic beats (e.g., “almost kiss,” big reconciliations). - Safe, symmetrical emotional arcs. We aim to embrace: - Emotional asymmetry (one character may change faster or more willingly than the other). - Subtle, character-specific moments of vulnerability. - A quiet, ambiguous ending that still feels emotionally true. - Supporting characters who challenge or reflect the leads, without existing solely to serve them. — # Chapter Planning Each chapter is approximately 1000 words, so each must focus tightly on a single key emotional or narrative movement. — CHAPTER 1: The Bookstore (First Meeting) Rhys ducks into a small, slightly cluttered bookstore to avoid a man with a camera. He’s flustered, wearing sunglasses, trying not to be recognized. Carys, at the counter, doesn’t look up from her book. She clocks who he is immediately but says nothing. Their conversation is awkward, clipped, and full of tension—he pushes charm, she gives flat, dry responses. He tries to impress her; she’s mildly amused but unimpressed. He buys a book he clearly doesn’t understand, mostly to stay longer. Key beat: Rhys feels thrown by her indifference. Carys, alone after he leaves, is clearly not as unaffected as she pretended. — CHAPTER 2: Return & Retaliation (Banter & Cracks) Rhys returns the next day under the pretense of buying a “part two.” She’s shelving books, pretends not to remember him. He’s more relaxed, leaning into the awkwardness of yesterday. They talk books—he tries to fake it, she calls him out. But he surprises her: he mentions a poem he memorized as a child. She’s thrown by the sincerity. He’s thrown by how much he wants her to like him. Key beat: Carys gives him a book—Dylan Thomas poems—saying “You might like these, or you might pretend you do.” He laughs but takes it seriously. — CHAPTER 3: The Poetry Night (First Spark) Rhys shows up at the local pub’s open mic poetry night, following Carys’ casual mention. She’s surprised—and a little pleased. The town notices him. Eleri, her friend, fangirls. Rhys is good-natured about it but awkward. He ends up reading a poem, vulnerably. It’s not a “big moment” but it’s real. On the walk home, they talk quietly—less performance, more truth. They stand outside the shop. There’s a moment, but they don’t kiss. They both feel it. Key beat: Carys watches him walk away. She touches the book of poems in her coat pocket. — CHAPTER 4: Close Quarters (Intimacy & Hesitation) Rhys visits again. It’s raining, the shop is quiet. They talk about their fathers—Carys’ ran the store; Rhys’ is estranged. She opens up slightly. He makes tea in the back while she restocks. They share a small, domestic moment. He touches her hand without thinking. She pulls away—not angrily, just startled. He apologizes. She says, “Don’t be sorry. Just don’t assume.” There’s tension, but not resentment. Key beat: Carys watches him leave again, conflicted. Rhys, walking home in the rain, doesn’t put his hood up. — CHAPTER 5: Exposure (Conflict & Retreat) A tabloid article leaks Rhys’ location—someone saw him at the pub. Paparazzi arrive in town. Carys’ shop is photographed. She’s furious. Rhys tries to apologize, but his tone shifts when she accuses him of bringing chaos. He says, “You liked me being here until it got complicated.” She says, “I liked you until you stopped being a person and became a headline.” They part on hurt, defensive words. Key beat: Rhys stands outside the shop later that night, but doesn’t go in. Carys watches from the upstairs window, but doesn’t come down. — CHAPTER 6: Unraveling (Self-Reckoning) Parallel scenes. Rhys alone in his rental cottage, on a call with his agent—offered a PR “rehabilitation” relationship and a new role. He’s visibly drained. He deletes the agent’s message. Carys with her Nan, who tells her she’s good at protecting herself—but sometimes at the cost of missing what’s right in front of her. Carys finds the Dylan Thomas book on the floor, rain-stained—left by Rhys during the argument. Key beat: Both characters sit alone in different rooms, holding the same silence, not knowing what comes next. — CHAPTER 7: The Goodbye (Open Wounds) Rhys comes back to the shop. Apologizes—not to fix things, but to be honest. He’s leaving. He says he doesn’t know who he is when people aren’t watching. Carys doesn’t reassure him. She just listens. She says, “You don’t have to be anything here. That’s why it scared you.” He smiles, small and sad. They part with a hug. It’s not romantic; it’s real. Neither says “maybe someday.” Key beat: Carys returns to her shelves. Outside, the rain has stopped. — CHAPTER 8: The Quiet Return (Unresolved Possibility) Weeks later. Spring. The store is busy again. Carys is shelving books. The door chime rings. Rhys, clean-shaven, in a plain jumper, holding a paper bag of pastries. No announcement, no grand gesture. He says, “I was driving by. Thought I’d see if you had the second part.” He’s referencing the bad book from Chapter 1. She smiles, despite herself. They don’t kiss. They shelve books. He says, “I got a place nearby. If you ever want to not talk about poetry.” She says, “That sounds exhausting.” He grins. It’s quiet, open-ended, real. Key beat: A book falls between them on the shelf. They both reach to pick it up—hands brush. — This plan preserves the emotional tension and chemistry the prompt demands, while avoiding tidy resolutions and over-polished romantic beats. It allows the story to unfold through layered, imperfect dialogue and meaningful silences—creating a human, grounded love story that lingers beyond the final page.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
Absolutely. Below are detailed, grounded explorations of the two central characters, designed to feel emotionally complex, specific, and human. These profiles aim to steer away from archetype and toward layered, breathing individuals who will carry the emotional weight of the story. — # Character 1: Rhys Morgan Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws: Rhys Morgan, 36, was born in a working-class family just outside Cardiff. His mother was a primary school teacher, his father a mechanic—strict, emotionally walled-off, and often absent even when present. Rhys was an only child, bookish in secret, charming in public. He discovered early that being likable—funny, flirtatious, magnetic—got him what he needed: attention, warmth, a sense of control. Acting was a natural extension of that. He left Wales at 18 to attend drama school in London, fueled by equal parts ambition and a need to escape. His early career was scrappy—TV dramas, soap operas—but he broke out in his late 20s in a romantic film that became a surprise hit. Since then he’s lived in the world of red carpets, press junkets, and curated personas. He’s used to being adored, which makes him deeply uncomfortable when he isn’t. His ego is porous—so much of it relies on the opinions of others. He has a habit of self-deprecating before others can do it for him, but it’s performative, not vulnerable. Idiosyncrasies: - He always carries a pen and scribbles on receipts, napkins—poetry fragments, impressions, lines of dialogue. - Can’t stand silence for too long—starts humming or tapping his fingers when things get too still. - Sleeps with the TV on, even if muted. White noise comforts him. - Occasionally quotes Shakespeare or Dylan Thomas at inappropriate times, usually to cover discomfort. Flaws: - Needs external validation like oxygen. - Avoids emotional intimacy through charm and deflection. - Easily defensive when he feels judged or unseen. - Can be manipulative without meaning to—adjusts his personality to suit who he’s with. Physicality: Rhys is tall (about 6’1"), lean but not gym-chiseled—more like someone who stays fit from movement and stress. His once-boyish looks are softening into something more lived-in: creases at the corners of his eyes, a faint scar on his chin from a stunt gone wrong. His hair is tousled, dark brown with a few premature streaks of grey that he hasn’t dyed, to his publicist’s dismay. He moves like someone who’s used to being watched—shoulders slightly back, smile at the ready—but there’s a tiredness in his body lately, as if the performance is heavier than it once was. In private, he slouches, rubs the back of his neck, touches his face when anxious. Motives, Allegiances, Relationships: Rhys is in Wales ostensibly for a break, but really to hide out after a PR scandal—nothing criminal, just messy and public: a toxic ex, a leaked video, false smiles on talk shows. He tells himself he’s waiting for things to die down, but he feels unmoored. His allegiance has always been to maintaining the persona—until now. He has a complicated relationship with his agent: loyal but increasingly resentful. He hasn’t spoken to his father in years. He keeps in touch with his mother, but their conversations are surface-level. He has no real close friends, just colleagues who like the version of him they know. What he wants—but doesn’t know how to name—is to be known, not liked. To find something, or someone, that feels uncurated. Quotes: - “People don’t really want the truth. They want the version of you that makes them feel something. I’ve just gotten very good at giving them that.” - Internal monologue: She didn’t even look up. Not even a flicker of recognition. It should’ve pissed me off. So why do I feel like I want to stay here until she does? — # Character 2: Carys Ellis Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws: Carys Ellis, 33, has lived in Llangrannog her whole life, except for three years at university in Aberystwyth, where she studied literature and quietly panicked at the idea of becoming someone else. Her father ran the local bookstore until his death five years ago. She came home to help with the shop “temporarily,” but never left. Partly because she loves it; partly because she doesn’t know what else to do. Carys is emotionally intelligent but guarded. She watches people closely, listens more than she talks, and rarely says exactly what she’s feeling. Her humor is dry, sometimes cutting, but rarely cruel. She values sincerity, resents performative charm, and is suspicious of people who seem too much. Idiosyncrasies: - Writes marginalia in her books—short, cryptic notes like “yes” or “no” or “ugh” or “hm” in pencil. - Keeps a small notebook of overheard conversations and odd customer interactions. - Has a ritual of putting on the kettle at exactly 3:00 p.m., even if she doesn’t drink the tea. - Loathes romcoms but secretly watches one every few months, then pretends she hated it. Flaws: - Can be emotionally withholding—protects herself by never fully investing. - Assumes people will leave or disappoint her, so she doesn’t give them the chance to prove otherwise. - Has a tendency to judge quickly, then feel guilty about it later. - Avoids risk, preferring the safety of her routines. Physicality: Carys is medium height (5’6"), pale, with dark auburn hair usually tied back messily. Freckles dust her nose and cheeks. She wears minimal makeup—mascara, on a fancy day. Her clothes are practical: jumpers, jeans, boots. There’s always a pen behind her ear or a bookmark in her sleeve. She moves with quiet efficiency. Not rushed, not showy. She rarely fidgets, but she will drum her fingers on the counter when frustrated. Her expressions are subtle—an arched eyebrow, a twitch of a smirk—but her eyes give her away when she’s flustered. Motives, Allegiances, Relationships: Carys is loyal to her town, her bookstore, and her small circle of people—her Nan, who raised her after her mother died young; Eleri, her effervescent best friend; and the regulars who come in for poetry recs and quiet conversation. She wants connection, but on her terms—and has spent years convincing herself that’s a reasonable boundary. She fears becoming irrelevant or unseen, even as she chooses to stay on the sidelines. Rhys disrupts that balance in ways that both irritate and excite her. Quotes: - “Just because you can say something beautifully doesn’t mean it’s true. Or useful.” - Internal monologue: He’s probably used to women giggling at his jokes. I’m not giggling. I’m laughing. Silently. On the inside. That’s different. — These two characters are designed to clash in ways that create friction, honesty, and eventually intimacy. Their flaws complement each other; their strengths challenge each other. Their voices are distinct, and their emotional arcs will be defined not by dramatic transformation, but by the quiet bravery of choosing to be seen.

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 1: The Bookstore The bell above the door gave a half-hearted chime as Rhys ducked inside, breathless and rain-damp. A gust of salt wind followed him in, tugging at the hem of his coat before the door swung shut behind him. For a moment, he just stood there, back pressed against the glass, chest rising and falling with the kind of theatrical urgency he usually reserved for chase scenes. The shop was quiet. Warm. Smelled like old paper and lemon polish. A small radio hummed something classical from behind the counter. The woman at the till didn’t look up. She was sitting on a stool, legs crossed beneath her, a paperback splayed in one hand. A mug of tea steamed beside her elbow. She turned a page with a practiced flick. Rhys cleared his throat. “Bit wild out there.” “Mm,” she said, not looking up. He stepped further in, brushing water from his hair. The shop was narrow and deep, shelves stretching all the way to the back, crooked with the weight of too many books. It was the sort of place that hadn’t changed in years. He liked it immediately, though he wouldn’t have admitted that out loud. “Do you have a back door?” he asked, glancing over his shoulder. “Just out of curiosity.” Now she looked up. Slowly. Cool eyes, dark grey or maybe blue—it was hard to tell in the lamplight. She gave him a quick once-over. Not lascivious. Just… assessing. “Is someone chasing you?” He smiled, charming. Automatically. “You never know.” She raised an eyebrow. “If you’re planning to rob the place, I should tell you we take in about fifteen pounds on a good day. And that’s if someone buys the fancy bookmarks.” “Tempting,” he said, stepping closer. “But no. Just avoiding someone with a camera, actually.” “Right,” she said. “Paparazzi?” He blinked. “That obvious?” “Not really.” She set her book down, spine-up. “But you’re wearing sunglasses in the rain, and you have the air of someone who expected to be recognized.” He laughed, but it caught awkwardly in his throat. “That’s brutal.” “Accurate, though,” she said, standing. “Can I help you find something, or should I just keep pretending I don’t know who you are?” He smiled again, slower this time. “Let’s go with pretending. It’s kind of refreshing.” She didn’t smile back, but there was a twitch at the corner of her mouth. “Fiction’s to your left. Biography behind you. Poetry’s in the back, but I wouldn’t recommend starting there unless you’re in a particularly self-destructive mood.” “Poetry, huh?” He wandered toward the back, trailing fingers along the spines. “You don’t strike me as the self-destructive type.” “I run a bookstore in a town with a population of eight hundred,” she called after him. “That’s either romantic or suicidal, depending on the weather.” He grinned. Picked up a slim volume at random and flipped through it without reading. “Do people actually buy poetry?” “Occasionally. Usually by accident.” He turned. “And what do you recommend? For a man on the run.” She tilted her head, considering. “Depends. Are you running from something or toward something?” “Bit of both,” he admitted. “Then you want something ambiguous. Try Murakami. Or the travel section.” He found his way back to the front and held out the book he’d grabbed. She looked at the cover. “Pablo Neruda?” she said, skeptical. “Why not?” She took it from him, flipped it open. “This one’s in Spanish.” He blinked. “Right.” She snapped it shut and handed it back. “You sure you’re not just here to loiter?” “Would it work if I said I love bookstores and just wanted to soak in the atmosphere?” “No.” He laughed. “Fair enough.” She rang up the book anyway, tapping keys with precise fingers. “That’ll be seven pounds.” He handed her a ten. She gave him change without making small talk. The radio switched to a piano concerto. “Do you work here alone?” he asked, stalling. “I own the place.” “Oh. Sorry. I didn’t mean—” “It’s fine.” She slid the book into a paper bag. “People assume.” “Carys,” she said, before he could ask. “It’s on the receipt.” He looked down. “Right. Carys. Pretty name.” “That’s the general consensus.” He tucked the book under his arm. “Well. Thanks for the shelter.” “Any time. Just try not to bring the press next time. I like being the town’s best-kept secret.” He paused at the door, hand on the knob. “You’re not going to ask for a selfie?” “No.” “Autograph?” “No.” “Not even a dramatic reading from my latest blockbuster?” She tilted her head. “I’d rather you read Neruda. In Spanish.” He laughed again, real this time. “Touché.” The bell jingled as he stepped outside. The rain had let up slightly, misting instead of pouring. He hesitated, looked back through the glass. She was already reading again, the steam from her mug curling around her like a barrier. He walked away, grinning and unsettled. Inside, Carys flipped the page, then paused. Looked at the door. After a moment, she picked up his receipt, smoothed it flat, and tucked it into her book as a bookmark.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a confident, well-executed opening chapter that largely delivers on both the writing prompt and the chapter plan. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. It moves with genuine wit and naturalism, avoiding the over-polished "scripted" quality the author warned against in their plan. Lines like "I run a bookstore in a town with a population of eight hundred. That's either romantic or suicidal, depending on the weather" feel character-specific rather than generic. The exchange about the Spanish Neruda is a small, well-observed comic beat that doesn't overstay its welcome. The banter has rhythm without feeling choreographed. Character consistency is strong. Rhys deploys charm reflexively and automatically—the narration notes his smile was "charming. Automatically."—which is exactly the profile's instruction that his charm is performative. His ego is gently pricked by her indifference, and his lingering at the door asking about selfies and autographs reads as someone who genuinely cannot process being dismissed. Carys is appropriately dry, efficient, and guarded, while the final beat—tucking his receipt into her book as a bookmark—is a lovely, understated reveal of her inner state without over-explaining it. The setting is economical and effective. "Old paper and lemon polish," the half-hearted bell, the crooked shelves—these details establish atmosphere without becoming purple or overwrought. The Welsh coastal town feels present without being labored. The chapter plan called for: Rhys feeling thrown by her indifference, Carys being secretly affected. Both are delivered. The plan also called for him buying a book he clearly doesn't understand—the Spanish Neruda is a clever, funny execution of this beat. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is competent and enjoyable but rarely surprising. The dynamic of "charming man meets unimpressed woman" is well-worn territory, and while the execution is above average, there are few moments that genuinely subvert expectation or offer a fresh angle. The "cool eyes, dark grey or maybe blue" description is a minor cliché. The observation that Rhys "liked it immediately, though he wouldn't have admitted that out loud" is a small tell-don't-show moment—the narration explaining his emotional state rather than letting it emerge. The ending is effective but slightly predictable: the receipt-as-bookmark is a good beat, but readers will see it coming from the moment she takes his receipt. It's earned but not surprising. The chapter is also somewhat safe in its emotional register. The plan promised "awkward, clipped" conversation "full of tension"—the actual dialogue is smoother and more charming than that description suggests. The tension is present but mild; it reads more as pleasant sparring than genuine discomfort. Rhys is thrown, but not deeply so. The "fragile ego" element from the prompt is present but could be more pronounced—we get a hint of it but not the full vulnerability the profile describes. The prose is clean and controlled throughout, with no significant purple passages or forced metaphors. The one slightly strained image—"steam from her mug curling around her like a barrier"—is the only moment where the metaphor feels a touch deliberate, though it's not egregious. Overall, this is a polished, enjoyable chapter that executes its brief competently. It's not a masterpiece, but it's genuinely good work: the dialogue is natural, the characters are distinct, and the emotional beats land. Its main limitation is that it plays it relatively safe within a familiar premise. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 2: Return & Retaliation The bell chimed again the next afternoon, softer this time, as if it too felt sheepish. Carys didn’t look up. “If you’re here to return the Neruda, I should warn you—still in Spanish.” Rhys laughed. “You wound me. I came back to thank you for the cultural enrichment. I’ve been whispering sonnets to my coffee all morning.” Carys raised her eyes slowly from her ledger. “And how’s your coffee responding?” “Bitter, but attentive.” She allowed herself the faintest smile. “Well. At least one of you’s consistent.” He leaned against the counter, a little too casual. Sunglasses tucked into the collar of his jumper this time. His hair was damp at the edges, curls softening in the humid spring air. He looked less like a movie star today. And a little more tired. “I forgot to get a receipt,” he said, gesturing vaguely toward the till. “You didn’t.” “Didn’t I?” She reached behind the counter and held up the exact receipt, still folded neatly. “You used it to buy my silence.” “Ah. So the bribe was unsuccessful.” “Afraid so.” He grinned, then let his eyes wander over the shelves. “I actually did want another book.” “Of course you did.” “I’m serious. I read the Neruda.” “You read the Dedication page.” “Barely,” he admitted. “But I did try. I even googled a translation.” She stood, brushing off her hands. “And?” He shrugged. “It made me feel... something. I don’t know what. Like I was missing something important.” “That’s the correct response to poetry.” “Confusion?” “Longing.” He looked at her carefully. “You always talk like that?” “Only when I’m being pretentious.” He chuckled and turned toward the nearest shelf. “Alright. No poetry. What would you give someone who’s... starting over?” Carys hesitated. “Starting over how?” He picked up a random hardcover, flipped it open, closed it again. “Let’s say they’ve been playing a role so long they forgot what their own voice sounds like.” She tilted her head. “Do you want the truth or a distraction?” “Dealer’s choice.” She walked to the fiction shelf and pulled out a slim paperback with a worn spine. “This one’s about a man who fakes his own death and hides in a lighthouse.” “Sounds uplifting.” “He ends up opening a bakery.” Rhys blinked. “...What?” “It’s better than it sounds.” He took the book from her. “You’re not trying to tell me something, are you?” “I don’t know. Do you like baking?” He laughed. “I burn toast.” She returned to the counter and sat, watching him flip through the pages. “You’re not what I expected.” “That makes two of us.” She studied him for a moment. “Why are you really here, Rhys?” He looked up. She didn’t blink. “I needed somewhere quiet.” “Plenty of quiet places in the world.” “This one had a bookstore. And you weren’t impressed by me.” “Still not.” He smiled, but there was something smaller behind it. “People usually are. Impressed, I mean. Or they pretend to be. It’s like a reflex.” “That doesn’t sound like a compliment.” “It’s not. I’ve spent so long being... likable. It’s a skill, you know.” “I believe it,” she said. “But it’s not the same as being known.” He looked down at the book in his hands. “No. It’s really not.” They were quiet for a moment. The radio was playing again—some folk song with fiddles and a low, gravelly voice. Outside, the wind had picked up. A gull cried somewhere distant. “Have you always lived here?” he asked. “Mostly. Left for university. Came back when my dad got sick.” “And stayed.” She nodded. “Do you ever want to leave again?” “Sometimes,” she said, then added with a shrug, “But I think I’m more afraid of wanting things than not having them.” He glanced at her. “That’s a line from a movie.” “No, it’s not.” “It should be.” She gave him a look. “Please don’t quote it in your next film.” “Too late. I’ve already claimed it.” They were quiet again. This time, it felt less like a pause and more like a space being allowed. Rhys set the book down. “Do you ever—” He stopped. Started again. “Would you want to—” Carys raised an eyebrow. “Spit it out.” “There’s a poetry night. At the pub. Tomorrow, I think.” “You’re inviting me to something I already go to?” He smirked. “I’m inviting myself, really. But I thought it might be less weird if I knew someone.” She studied him. “You’re not going to stand up and recite anything, are you?” “Only if I’m feeling particularly self-destructive.” Her lips twitched. “Alright. But don’t sit at my table.” “Wouldn’t dream of it.” He picked up the lighthouse-bakery book and tapped it against the counter. “I’ll take this one.” “Eight pounds.” He handed her a ten. She gave him change. Their fingers brushed—brief, but enough to register. He didn’t comment. Neither did she. He took the paper bag, nodded once. “See you tomorrow.” “Mm.” The bell chimed as he left. Carys watched the door for a moment, then opened her ledger again. The receipt from yesterday was still there, tucked between pages. She smoothed it, folded it again, and slipped it into the back of her notebook. She didn’t know why. But she left it there.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 of this story demonstrates considerable craft and a strong command of dialogue-driven character work. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions. DIALOGUE AND VOICE: The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. Both characters have distinct, consistent voices. Rhys's lines carry that performative charm with a slightly hollow quality underneath—"Bitter, but attentive" is a good example of his wit that also reveals his self-awareness about his own bitterness. Carys's responses are clipped, dry, and precise without feeling artificially witty. The exchange about the receipt ("You used it to buy my silence" / "Afraid so") is genuinely good—it's economical and character-revealing. The dialogue rarely feels scripted or over-polished, which was a stated goal. The "longing" exchange about poetry is perhaps the most elegant moment: brief, thematically resonant, and earned. CHARACTERIZATION: Both characters are consistent with their profiles. Rhys's fragile ego surfaces naturally—"People usually are. Impressed, I mean. Or they pretend to be. It's like a reflex"—and his self-awareness about his own charm is rendered without being too on-the-nose. Carys's emotional guardedness is shown through her clipped responses and the small, telling action at the end (keeping the receipt). The line "I think I'm more afraid of wanting things than not having them" is a strong character moment, though Rhys's response ("That's a line from a movie") risks being too clever—it deflects from the emotional weight of what she said, which is both in-character and slightly frustrating as a reader. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT: The chapter builds genuine warmth and tension. The finger-brush moment is handled with admirable restraint—neither character comments on it, which is exactly right. The ending, with Carys keeping the receipt, is quietly effective. The chapter earns its lightness through the banter while maintaining the undercurrent of something more serious. CHAPTER PLAN ADHERENCE: The plan called for Rhys returning under pretense, pretending not to remember him (though this is reversed—she remembers him immediately, which is actually better), him trying to fake knowledge of books, her calling him out, and a moment of sincerity (the poem memory is replaced by his admission about longing). The Dylan Thomas book is given here rather than the Neruda, which is a slight deviation—the plan specified Dylan Thomas poems as the gift, but instead she gives him a lighthouse-bakery novel. The poetry night invitation is included. Overall, the plan is followed in spirit if not in every detail. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is not without flaws. "Like I was missing something important" is a slightly generic emotional beat. The lighthouse-bakery book is a charming invention but the metaphor (man faking his death, hiding, then opening a bakery) is a little too on-the-nose as a parallel to Rhys's situation—it risks being the kind of "clever" that draws attention to the author's hand. The folk song with "fiddles and a low, gravelly voice" is a minor atmospheric cliché. The line "A gull cried somewhere distant" is the most egregious example of atmospheric shorthand—it's the kind of detail that signals "coastal melancholy" without earning it. These are minor issues in an otherwise strong chapter. CREATIVITY: The chapter avoids most romantic clichés. The inversion of who invites whom to the poetry night is a nice touch. The receipt as a recurring object is a good choice for grounding the emotional continuity. PURPLE PROSE / FORCED METAPHOR: Largely absent. The writing is clean and restrained. The gull line is the closest thing to purple prose, and it's brief. OVERALL: A strong chapter that delivers on the promise of the plan and the character profiles. The dialogue is the standout element, and the emotional beats are handled with appropriate subtlety. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 3: The Poetry Night The pub was already warm with bodies and firelight by the time Rhys arrived, hood pulled low against the evening drizzle. It was louder than he expected—half the town seemed to be there, crammed into mismatched chairs or leaning against the bar, pints in hand, cheeks flushed from beer and laughter. He paused just inside the door, blinking in the low light, letting his eyes adjust. The air smelled of damp wool, hops, and something fried. No one paid him much attention. Good. He spotted Carys in the far corner, sitting at a small round table with a woman who looked like a storm in human form—bright lipstick, loud laugh, hands that talked as much as her mouth. Eleri, maybe. She was gesturing animatedly with a glass of wine, and Carys was doing her usual half-smile, eyes kind but distant. He didn’t wave. Just found a spot near the back—standing room only—and ordered a pint from the bar. The MC, a lanky man in a cardigan with a voice like warm gravel, stepped up to the mic. “Evening, folks. Thanks for dragging yourselves out on this fine Welsh night. We’ve got a good list tonight. Some newcomers, some old favourites. First up, we’ve got Glyn—who insists this one’s not about his ex-wife, but we all know better.” Laughter rippled through the room. Rhys sipped his beer and watched. Glyn was nervous but funny, reading a bitter little poem about a stolen kettle that somehow turned into a metaphor for heartbreak. The crowd loved it. Carys clapped politely. Eleri whooped. Rhys leaned against the wall and let himself settle. By the third reader, he was half-listening, half-watching her. Carys had her arms folded loosely across her chest, one boot tucked behind the other, head tilted just slightly. Her expression didn’t change much, but he could tell when she liked something—her eyes narrowed, just a little, like she was listening harder. When the fifth reader stepped down, the MC returned with a grin. “Alright, bit of a surprise tonight. We’ve got someone new. Rhys Morgan, would you do us the honour?” Every head turned. Rhys froze mid-sip. Carys looked over, startled. Eleri clapped enthusiastically. “Yes! Go on, movie star!” He smiled, thinly, and walked to the mic before he could talk himself out of it. His heart thudded against his ribs. He hadn’t been on a stage this small in years. The mic stand wobbled as he adjusted it. He cleared his throat. “Hi,” he said, voice lower than usual. “I’m Rhys. I… didn’t plan to read anything tonight, but Glyn inspired me with his kettle-based heartbreak, so here we are.” Laughter. Kind, not mocking. He reached into his coat pocket and pulled out a napkin. Unfolded it carefully. His handwriting was cramped and messy, the ink slightly smudged. “This isn’t from a film,” he said. “Just something I wrote. Don’t judge too harshly.” He read. The poem was short. Not polished. It was about noise—about how silence can be louder than applause, and how sometimes the quiet places are the ones that echo longest. When he finished, no one spoke for a moment. Then came soft, genuine applause. A few murmurs of appreciation. He stepped down quickly, heart hammering, and made a beeline for the bar. Ordered another pint with shaking fingers. “Didn’t know you wrote poetry,” said a voice beside him. Carys. “Neither did I,” he said. “I usually just write nonsense on napkins.” “It wasn’t nonsense.” He glanced at her. “You came over. That’s either very good or very bad.” She shrugged, eyes still on the stage. “I figured hiding back there was poor form.” He smiled. “How’d I do?” “You didn’t embarrass yourself.” “High praise.” They stood side by side for a moment, watching the next reader—a teenage girl with nervous hands and a voice like wind through trees. “I wasn’t planning to read,” he said quietly. “I believe you.” “I don’t even know why I came.” “Yes, you do.” He looked at her. Her gaze didn’t waver. “You wanted to be somewhere you weren’t performing.” He swallowed. “Did I succeed?” She shrugged. “Almost.” They didn’t talk for a while. The girl finished her poem, blushed furiously, and was met with warm applause. Carys finished her wine. “I should go.” He nodded. “I’ll walk you.” She hesitated, then nodded once. They stepped out into the night. The rain had stopped, but the streets were slick and shining. The sound of waves echoed in the distance. They walked in silence for a bit, boots scuffing against stone. The town was quiet now, the pub’s noise left behind. “Do you miss it?” she asked suddenly. “Miss what?” “The noise. The cameras. The attention.” He thought. “Some part of me does. Or thinks I should. It’s like… I spent so long being grateful for it, I forgot to ask if I liked it.” She nodded. “What about you?” he asked. “Do you ever wish people saw you more?” She gave him a sideways glance. “People see what they want. I’ve made peace with that.” They reached the bookstore. The sign swayed gently in the wind. Carys turned to face him. “Thanks for walking me.” He hesitated. “Can I—?” She raised an eyebrow. He smiled, a little sheepish. “Never mind.” She stepped back, unlocking the door. “Goodnight, Rhys.” “Goodnight, Carys.” She slipped inside. The door clicked shut behind her. Rhys stood there for a moment, staring at the faint light in the window. Then he turned and walked into the night, hands in his pockets, the echo of his own poem ringing quietly in his ears.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 represents a competent but somewhat uneven execution of the chapter plan. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter largely follows its plan: Rhys attends the poetry night, reads a poem vulnerably, they walk home together, there's a moment that doesn't resolve into a kiss, and Carys is left with quiet feeling. The structural bones are solid. The dialogue is generally the strongest element. Lines like "You didn't embarrass yourself" / "High praise" capture the dynamic well—Carys's dry deflection, Rhys's need for approval. "You wanted to be somewhere you weren't performing" / "Did I succeed?" / "Almost" is genuinely good: it's economical, character-consistent, and carries subtext without over-explaining. The exchange about missing the noise and cameras is also reasonably naturalistic. Character consistency is mostly maintained. Rhys's nervousness at the mic, his shaking fingers, his "Can I—?" that he abandons—these are consistent with a fragile ego and someone genuinely thrown off his game. Carys's restraint, her sideways glances, her efficiency in ending the evening—all consistent with her profile. WEAKNESSES: The chapter plan called for Eleri to "fangirl," which happens only briefly and superficially. Eleri is described as "a storm in human form" which is a slightly strained metaphor, though not egregious. The plan also called for Rhys to be "good-natured but awkward" about the town noticing him—this is handled adequately but quickly. The poem Rhys reads is described rather than shown. This is a significant missed opportunity. We're told it's "about noise—about how silence can be louder than applause, and how sometimes the quiet places are the ones that echo longest." This is vague and somewhat clichéd as a description. Showing even a few lines of the actual poem would have been far more powerful and would have demonstrated rather than asserted its emotional impact. The decision to summarize the poem rather than render it is a clear instance of tell-don't-show. "A voice like wind through trees" for the teenage girl is a pleasant but unremarkable simile. "A voice like warm gravel" for the MC is similarly functional but not distinctive. The ending—Rhys standing outside, "the echo of his own poem ringing quietly in his ears"—is a bit too neat and slightly purple. It's the kind of closing image that sounds resonant but is actually somewhat generic. The chapter plan specified that Carys "touches the book of poems in her coat pocket" as the key beat. This doesn't happen in the chapter. The key beat as written (Carys returning inside, Rhys walking away) is less specific and less emotionally precise than what was planned. The chapter is competent and readable, but it doesn't quite achieve the emotional depth it aims for. The dialogue carries it, but the narrative prose is occasionally reaching for significance without fully earning it. The "almost" moment is handled with appropriate restraint, but the overall emotional temperature feels slightly muted rather than charged with genuine tension. The Eleri character is underused—she whoops and then disappears, which wastes the opportunity for her to complicate or reflect the dynamic between the leads. Overall: a solid, readable chapter that executes its plan adequately but misses several opportunities for greater specificity and emotional depth. The dialogue is the clear strength; the narrative prose and the decision to summarize rather than show the poem are the clearest weaknesses. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 12 Compelling Plot: 11 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 9 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 10 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 12 Characters Consistent with Profile: 14 Followed Chapter Plan: 12 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 14

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 4: Close Quarters The rain came early and without warning the next day—a cold, steady downpour that turned the high street slick and reflective. By late afternoon, the town was quiet, the kind of grey hush that made even the shop signs look subdued. Rhys showed up at the bookstore just before closing, his hood pulled low, jeans damp at the cuffs. He tapped the glass gently, then pushed the door open without waiting for a reply. Carys looked up from the counter. She didn’t smile, but she didn’t tell him to leave, either. “You’re dripping,” she said, eyeing the puddle forming around him. “Seems to be my thing lately.” He pulled off his coat, shook it outside the door, and hung it on the stand near the entrance. His jumper clung to him in places, darker where the rain had gotten through. “You’re late,” she added. “For what?” “For no longer pretending you’re not going to keep coming here.” He grinned, sheepish. “Guilty.” She gestured toward the back. “There’s tea. If you don’t mind mismatched mugs.” He followed her behind the counter, through a narrow doorway into a cramped back room. It smelled of paper and bergamot, old wood and something faintly herbal. A kettle was already boiling. The space was barely big enough for two people to stand without brushing elbows. A tiny table sat beneath a window streaked with rain. Books were stacked on every flat surface. Carys moved with practiced ease, pulling two mugs from a shelf. One read: “Books Before Boys.” The other had a faded cartoon sheep and a chip in the rim. “You get many visitors back here?” Rhys asked, ducking slightly beneath a low beam. “Just Nan, sometimes. And Eleri when she’s had a bad date.” “Should I be flattered or concerned?” She poured the tea. “Depends. Do you count as a bad date?” He accepted the mug with a crooked smile. “Too soon to tell.” They sat opposite each other, knees almost touching under the table. The rain drummed steadily on the roof above them. “You were good last night,” she said, after a beat. He looked up. “At the poetry?” She nodded. “I didn’t think you’d say that.” “I didn’t think I would either.” He laughed softly. “It felt… strange. Saying something real in front of people.” “You do that for a living, don’t you?” He shook his head. “Acting isn’t real. It’s pretending very convincingly. That’s different.” “What about here?” she asked. He met her eyes. “I haven’t quite figured that out yet.” She looked down at her tea. “It’s hard, isn’t it? Saying things that matter.” “Terrifying,” he said. “But you seem good at it. You say exactly what you mean.” “No,” she said. “I just say what I’m sure about. Which isn’t much.” They were quiet again. The silence between them was never uncomfortable, but it was charged—like a held breath. Rhys set his mug down. “Can I ask you something?” “Depends.” “Why haven’t you asked me to leave yet?” She considered. “Do you want to be asked to leave?” “No.” “Then maybe I’m just being polite.” He gave her a look. She exhaled, then said, carefully, “I like having you here. Even when it’s inconvenient.” “That’s… not the warmest thing anyone’s ever said to me. But I’ll take it.” A faint smile touched her lips. “You’re not used to people being honest with you, are you?” “Not like this, no.” “What’s it usually like?” He looked down. “Smiles that don’t reach eyes. Conversations that feel like interviews. People who want something, or want to be seen wanting me.” “And what do you want?” He hesitated. “I think I’m still figuring that out.” Carys stood, suddenly restless, and moved to a nearby shelf. She started reshelving a few stray paperbacks, fingers moving quickly. “You don’t have to have an answer,” she said. “But I want one,” he said. “For once.” She didn’t turn around. “You’re not the only one hiding, you know.” “Are you?” She glanced over her shoulder. “I live in a town where nothing ever happens. I run a shop that barely breaks even. I read books about people making choices I’m too scared to make. So yes. I’m hiding too.” He stood and stepped closer, slowly, as if not to spook her. She turned to face him. They were barely a foot apart now. “I meant to kiss you last night,” he said. “I know.” “I didn’t because it felt like… it would be something I couldn’t take back.” She nodded. “And now?” He looked at her—really looked. She wasn’t wearing makeup. A loose strand of hair curled behind her ear. Her eyes were tired but clear. “Now I’m still not sure if I should.” She held his gaze. “Then don’t. Not unless you’re sure.” He reached out without thinking, let his fingers brush hers where they rested on a shelf. She didn’t pull away. But she didn’t lean in, either. They stood like that, the rain pattering overhead, the books around them quietly bearing witness. Eventually, she stepped back. “The shop closes in ten minutes.” He nodded. “Right.” She walked past him, back to the front. He followed, slower. At the counter, he picked up the chipped mug and turned it in his hand. “You know… I think I prefer it here.” “In the shop?” “In this town. In the quiet.” “Careful,” she said. “You’ll ruin your reputation.” He smiled. “Maybe it needs ruining.” She didn’t reply. As he pulled on his coat, she said, “You’re welcome back. If you want.” He paused at the door. “I always want.” The bell jingled softly behind him as he stepped into the rain. Carys watched the door for a long time before turning out the lights.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 of this slow-burn romance is a competent, largely successful execution of its stated plan. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. The exchange feels genuinely naturalistic—imperfect, elliptical, with characters talking around things rather than at them. Lines like "I like having you here. Even when it's inconvenient" and "Not unless you're sure" carry real weight without being overwrought. The voices are distinct: Rhys is warmer, more openly searching; Carys is clipped, precise, guarded. The rhythm of their conversation—short exchanges, silences, deflections—feels earned rather than constructed. The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The domestic intimacy (tea, cramped back room), the conversation about fathers (though this is handled more obliquely than planned—Carys mentions hiding but doesn't explicitly discuss her father), the hand-touch moment, and the "don't assume" beat are all present, though the plan's specific line "Don't be sorry. Just don't assume" is replaced by something slightly different but thematically consistent. Character consistency is strong. Rhys's need for validation, his self-awareness about performance versus reality, his impulsive physical gesture—all track with his profile. Carys's guardedness, her dry wit, her admission of hiding, her refusal to lean in—all consistent. The setting works quietly. The cramped back room, rain on the roof, mismatched mugs—these details are specific without being labored. WEAKNESSES: The chapter occasionally tips into telling rather than showing. "The silence between them was never uncomfortable, but it was charged—like a held breath" is a direct authorial intrusion that explains what the dialogue and action should be conveying on their own. Similarly, "the books around them quietly bearing witness" is a slightly precious touch—the kind of light personification that borders on purple prose. The chapter plan called for Rhys to touch her hand "without thinking" and for her to pull away—"not angrily, just startled"—followed by his apology and her line "Don't be sorry. Just don't assume." The actual chapter softens this: she doesn't pull away, there's no apology, and the tension is more mutual and romantic than the plan's slightly more fraught version. This is a minor deviation but worth noting—the plan's version had more friction; the execution is smoother and slightly more conventionally romantic. The "I meant to kiss you last night" exchange is the chapter's most overtly romantic beat, and while it's handled with restraint, it does feel like a slight departure from the plan's intention to avoid "familiar romantic beats." The almost-kiss is exactly the kind of beat the writing plan explicitly said to avoid ("almost kiss"). The chapter doesn't quite deliver a kiss, but it stages the moment in a fairly conventional way. Some lines are slightly too neat. "Maybe it needs ruining" is a bit too polished—it sounds like scripted dialogue rather than something a person would actually say in that moment. Similarly, "I always want" as a parting line is effective but slightly too cinematic. The chapter's emotional arc is clear and satisfying, but perhaps a touch too smooth. The plan called for "tension, but not resentment" after the hand moment—the chapter delivers something closer to mutual romantic tension, which is warmer and less complicated than intended. The Carys interiority promised in the plan (watching him leave, conflicted; Rhys walking in the rain without his hood) is present but compressed. The final image—Carys watching the door before turning out the lights—is good, but the plan's specific image of Rhys not putting his hood up is absent, which was a quietly powerful character beat. OVERALL: This is a well-crafted chapter with strong dialogue, consistent characterization, and genuine emotional warmth. Its weaknesses are mostly in slight over-smoothing of the intended friction and a few moments of authorial intrusion. It's above average but not exceptional—competent and affecting without being truly surprising or distinctive. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 14

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 5: Exposure The first sign was a photo on Eleri’s phone. She burst into the shop just before noon, cheeks pink from the wind, holding her mobile like it was burning her fingers. “You’ve seen this, right?” Carys looked up from the till. “Good morning to you too.” Eleri dropped the phone on the counter. “I mean—look. Look at that.” The screen showed a grainy photo of Rhys, unmistakably him, standing outside the pub, backlit by the warm glow of the windows. A pint in one hand, his face turned slightly to the side. He looked… normal. Tired. Human. The headline above it read: “Rhys Morgan Hiding Out in Coastal Village? Fans Spot Star at Local Pub’s Poetry Night.” Carys read it twice, slowly. Beneath the photo, a few lines speculated about a “mystery woman” and linked to another article about his recent PR fallout. The tone was predictably breathless. Eleri leaned in. “Did you know? Before this?” “No,” Carys said. Eleri gave her a look. “Come on.” “I didn’t.” Eleri blinked. “Well. Shit.” The bell jingled. A man stepped in—camera slung around his neck, raincoat still damp. He glanced around the shop, eyes lingering too long before moving on. Carys stood straighter. “Can I help you?” He smiled, but it was thin and practiced. “Just browsing. Thanks.” He drifted toward the fiction section, clearly not reading anything. Carys turned back to Eleri and lowered her voice. “That’s the second one today.” Eleri’s eyes widened. “You’re kidding.” “No. First one came in this morning asking if we carried screenplays. He had a mic clipped to his shirt.” Eleri whistled low. “They’re here for him.” “Yeah,” Carys said. “They are.” She picked up Eleri’s phone and closed the photo. Her hands were tighter than they needed to be. — Rhys showed up just before closing, as usual. He looked tired, but not surprised. His jumper was darker than usual, and he wore a beanie low over his forehead. He had the look of someone prepared to be seen and trying not to be. Carys didn’t speak right away. She was wiping down the counter with short, efficient strokes. “I saw it,” she said finally. He nodded. Didn’t bother pretending. “Wasn’t me,” he said. “I didn’t tip anyone off.” “Then who did?” He hesitated. “Someone at the pub, maybe. Or someone saw me on the street. It doesn’t take much.” She didn’t reply. He stepped closer. “I didn’t want this.” “No,” she said. “But it always follows you, doesn’t it?” He flinched, just slightly. “I didn’t think it’d happen here.” “That’s the problem, Rhys. You didn’t think.” Silence, heavy. He said, quieter, “I’ve tried to keep it away from you.” She looked at him. “You can’t keep it away. You are it.
